one_line_takeaway: A benchmark-driven demonstration that swapping ECDSA for ML-DSA-65 signatures in blockchain-verified federated learning preserves model accuracy and adds negligible relative compute overhead (<0.2% of on-chain transaction time), with the real cost being ~47x larger signatures and ~9x higher (estimated) verification gas.
key_finding: Across MNIST/SVHN/HAR, ML-DSA-65 keeps sub-millisecond signing (~0.65 ms) and verification (~0.53 ms) with a fixed 3309-byte signature, and the choice of signature scheme barely affects FL accuracy (MNIST 3-client: PQC 99.19% vs ECDSA 99.07% vs NONE 99.32%). The central result is the overhead-ratio analysis: on-chain PQC signing+verification is only 0.017%-0.19% of total transaction time (vs 2.3% in the NoBC fixed-delay case), because blockchain latency dominates. The real cost of PQC is data/gas, not compute: signatures are ~47x larger than ECDSA (3309 B vs 71 B) and estimated verification gas is ~9x higher (~1.72x10^6 vs ~1.89x10^5 units). Round time grows sub-linearly with clients (9.01 s -> 10.30 s -> 14.85 s for 3/10/30 clients on MNIST-PQC-BC), and enabling blockchain adds ~31% to round time (6.88 s -> 9.01 s).
core_contribution: PQS-BFL is an end-to-end framework that hardens federated-learning (FL) update verification against quantum adversaries by (1) authenticating each client model update with ML-DSA-65 (FIPS 204, formerly Dilithium) post-quantum signatures instead of classical ECDSA, and (2) recording/verifying the signed update hashes on a blockchain smart contract for immutable, decentralized validation. The authors' own contribution is the integration architecture plus a comprehensive empirical characterization (crypto timings, key/sig sizes, gas, transaction latency, FL accuracy, scalability) comparing ML-DSA-65 against an ECDSA baseline and a no-signature (hash-only) baseline across three datasets. Their headline empirical claim is that once verification happens on-chain, the PQC computation is a negligible fraction (<0.2%) of end-to-end transaction time, so PQC is not the bottleneck. An open-source implementation is released. Note: the PQC-scheme comparison table (ML-DSA-65 vs Falcon-512 vs SPHINCS+) and the FL/PQC/blockchain background are the authors' benchmarking and framing; the FedAvg algorithm, secure-aggregation/DP/homomorphic-encryption techniques, and prior PQC-BFL works (Gurung et al., Gharavi et al./PQBFL, Ding et al.) are cited Related Work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: A Quantum Polynomial-Time (QPT) adversary that can passively observe all blockchain and interceptable off-chain traffic; actively inject, modify, or drop network messages; adaptively corrupt a subset of FL clients (obtaining their PQC secret keys); interact with the smart contract; and attempt to forge signatures on update hashes for uncorrupted clients or manipulate aggregation. Security goals: update authenticity, update integrity, and unforgeability. Guarantees rest on three assumptions: EUF-CMA security of ML-DSA-65 against QPT adversaries (grounded in MLWE/MSIS hardness, NIST Level 2), collision/second-preimage resistance of SHA3-256 against QPT adversaries, and blockchain consensus/finality/immutability. The paper is honest that Assumption 3 is strong: if the underlying ledger still signs transactions with classical crypto (e.g., ECDSA), the ledger itself remains quantum-vulnerable, so PQS-BFL secures the FL update layer but does NOT deliver end-to-end quantum resistance without a PQC-secured blockchain.
limitations: The authors' own limitations plus reviewer-noted ones: (1) On-chain PQC verification gas is ESTIMATED, not measured from a working Solidity verifier or precompile, so the ~9x gas figure is an approximation. (2) Blockchain is a local Ganache simulation, bypassing real network latency/variability, so absolute transaction times (0.03-0.37 s) and the ~31% blockchain overhead are optimistic. (3) High PQC gas (~1.7x10^6 per update) could be a hard cost barrier on public mainnets. (4) Scalability tested only to 30 clients; throughput/verification/aggregator bottlenecks at hundreds-to-thousands untested. (5) Blockchain state grows with every recorded update hash. (6) Only ML-DSA-65 was integrated (Falcon/SPHINCS+ only micro-benchmarked, not run in the full pipeline). (7) Single centralized aggregator; simplified aggregation with no secure-aggregation/DP privacy layer, so gradient-leakage/inference privacy is out of scope. (8) The framework secures the FL update layer but not the ledger's own transaction signing, so it is not truly end-to-end quantum-resistant as deployed.

datasets_used: MNIST (28x28 grayscale digits, 10 classes; LeNet-style CNN ~21,800 params; non-IID Dirichlet alpha=0.5)
SVHN (32x32 color house numbers, 10 classes; custom CNN ~237,600 params; non-IID)
HAR (UCI Human Activity Recognition, 561 features, 6 classes; MLP ~42,900 params; non-IID)
quantitative_results: ML-DSA-65: KeyGen ~0.15 ms, Sign 0.656 ms, Verify 0.536 ms, signature 3309 B, public key 1952 B, private key 4032 B
ECDSA (SECP256k1): Sign 0.163 ms, Verify 0.145 ms, signature 71 B, public key 178 B, private key 241 B (PQC signing ~4x slower, verify ~3.6x slower than ECDSA)
MNIST 3-client accuracy: PQC 99.19%, ECDSA 99.07%, NONE 99.32%; MNIST-PQC-30c 98.818% (matches abstract's >98.8%)
HAR-PQC-3c accuracy 94.905% (round 3.05 s); SVHN-PQC-3c accuracy 93.819% (round 16.30 s)
Estimated on-chain verification gas: PQC ~1.7241x10^6 units vs ECDSA ~1.889x10^5 vs NONE ~1.7365x10^5 (~9x higher for PQC)
Transaction confirmation time (3-client): PQC 0.32-0.37 s vs ECDSA 0.12 s vs NONE 0.11 s
Crypto overhead ratio (sign+verify / tx time): PQC+BC 0.000167-0.001939 (0.017%-0.19%); ECDSA+BC 0.000380; PQC+NoBC 0.023099 (2.3%)
Scalability (MNIST-PQC-BC round time): 9.01 s (3 clients) -> 10.30 s (10 clients) -> 14.85 s (30 clients), sub-linear
Blockchain integration overhead: round time 6.88 s (NoBC) -> 9.01 s (BC), ~31% increase; overhead ratio dropped 2.31% -> 0.08%
PQC-scheme micro-benchmark (Table I, liboqs): ML-DSA-65 sign 0.656/verify 0.536 ms, 3309 B; Falcon-512 sign 3.282/verify 0.301 ms, 666 B; SPHINCS+-SHA2-128s sign 131.926/verify 3.637 ms, 7856 B
baselines_compared: ECDSA (SECP256k1) with SHA-256 pre-hash (classical signature baseline)
NONE (SHA3-256 / SHA-256 hashing only, no digital signature)
NoBC configuration (fixed 0.05 s delay in place of on-chain transaction) vs BC (on-chain verification)
Alternative PQC signature schemes micro-benchmarked but not integrated end-to-end: Falcon-512, SPHINCS+-SHA2-128s
